subject: Constitutional Law
module_title: Fundamental Rights III: Property, Religion and Directive Principles

questions.question: Article 44.1 provides that the State acknowledges that the homage of public worship is due to Almighty God and shall hold His Name in reverence. What is the legal status of this provision?

options.A: Art. 44.1 requires the State to fund Catholic religious education in all schools
options.B: Art. 44.1 is a preamble-like acknowledgment of religion; Irish courts have held it to be non-justiciable — it does not confer enforceable legal rights or duties
options.C: Art. 44.1 requires all State institutions to open proceedings with a Christian prayer
options.D: Art. 44.1 establishes Catholicism as the official State religion of Ireland

questions.explanation: Art. 44.1 is widely considered non-justiciable — it is an aspirational acknowledgment rather than a provision conferring enforceable individual rights. The 1972 Fifth Amendment removed the 'special position' of the Catholic Church (former Art. 44.1.2°) and the recognition of other named religious denominations. The remaining text of Art. 44.1 has been treated by the courts as a declaration of principle rather than a justiciable right. It does not constitute an establishment of religion and has no direct effect on what the State may or may not do regarding religious matters.

questions.question: Article 44.2.1° guarantees freedom of conscience and the free profession and practice of religion, subject to public order and morality. Which case established that this provision is directly justiciable and creates enforceable individual rights?

options.B: Campaign to Separate Church and State v Minister for Education [1998] 3 IR 321
options.C: McGrath and O'Ruairc v Trustees of Maynooth College [1979] ILRM 166
options.D: Re Article 26 and the Employment Equality Bill 1996 [1997] 2 IR 321

questions.explanation: McGrath and O'Ruairc v Trustees of Maynooth College [1979] ILRM 166 confirmed that Art. 44.2.1° creates directly enforceable individual rights. The case involved priests expelled from Maynooth who claimed interference with their religious rights. The court recognised Art. 44.2.1° as justiciable — it confers an enforceable constitutional right to freedom of conscience and practice of religion. Quinn's Supermarket [1972] is more specifically an Art. 40.1 equality case (regarding the Jewish traders exemption), which also engaged Art. 44 tangentially.

questions.question: Article 44.2.2° provides that the State shall not impose any disabilities or make any discrimination on the ground of religious profession, belief or status. In Quinn's Supermarket v AG [1972] IR 1, legislation exempting Jewish-operated shops from Sunday trading restrictions was challenged. What did the Supreme Court hold?

options.A: The exemption for Jewish traders violated Art. 44.2.2° as it discriminated in favour of a particular religion
options.B: The exemption for Jewish traders was constitutionally permissible — Art. 44.2.2° prohibits the State from imposing disabilities on grounds of religion; affirmatively accommodating religious practice by exemption does not violate the non-discrimination principle and may be required by the Art. 44.2.1° freedom of religion guarantee
options.C: The exemption was unconstitutional as all traders must be treated identically under trading hours legislation
options.D: The exemption violated Art. 40.1 equality and could not be justified by Art. 44

questions.explanation: Quinn's Supermarket v AG [1972] IR 1 upheld the exemption for Jewish-operated shops from Sunday trading restrictions (allowing them to open on Sundays since they were closed on Saturdays for the Sabbath). The Supreme Court held that this exemption was a constitutionally permissible accommodation of religious practice under Art. 44. The Art. 44.2.2° non-discrimination clause prohibits imposing disabilities on grounds of religion — it does not prevent the State from accommodating religious difference. The equality principle in Art. 40.1 permits recognition of genuine differences in circumstance.

questions.question: Article 44.2.3° prohibits the State from endowing any religion. Does State funding of Catholic denominational schools constitute an unconstitutional endowment?

options.A: Yes — any State funding that goes to a school with a Catholic ethos is an unconstitutional endowment under Art. 44.2.3°
options.B: No — Art. 44.2.5° expressly provides that the State may give assistance to denominational schools and is not prevented from providing financial support; the combination of Art. 44.2.3° (no endowment) and Art. 44.2.5° (permitted financial assistance) means that operational funding of denominational schools to provide the constitutionally mandated free primary education is permissible
options.C: State funding of any religious school is unconstitutional; only secular schools may receive State funding
options.D: Denominational school funding is an unconstitutional endowment but has been permitted by estoppel through long practice

questions.explanation: Art. 44.2.3° prohibits 'endowment' of any religion — understood as funding religious activity as such (e.g., directly funding a diocese, paying for Mass). Art. 44.2.5° expressly carves out an exception: 'Every religious denomination shall have the right to manage its own affairs... and to maintain institutions for educational or charitable purposes, and the State shall not endow any school which is not in accordance with the terms of this Act.' Most importantly, Art. 44.2.5° is read with s.76 of the Education Act as permitting State grants to denominational schools to fund education (a civic purpose), not religion per se.

questions.question: Article 44.2.6° provides that the property of any religious denomination or any educational institution shall not be divested save for necessary works of public utility and on payment of compensation. How does this provision relate to Art. 43 (private property)?

options.A: Art. 44.2.6° is entirely separate from Art. 43 and provides additional protection for religious property beyond the general property guarantee
options.B: Art. 44.2.6° and Art. 43 overlap in protecting property rights; Art. 44.2.6° provides specific additional protection for church and educational institution property — requiring both necessity for public utility and compensation for compulsory acquisition, mirroring but reinforcing the Art. 43 framework
options.C: Art. 44.2.6° prevails over Art. 43 — religious bodies cannot rely on Art. 43 for property protection
options.D: Art. 44.2.6° only applies to Catholic Church property; other religious denominations rely on Art. 43

questions.explanation: Art. 44.2.6° provides specific constitutional protection for the property of religious denominations and educational institutions — requiring (i) that any divestiture be for necessary public utility works, and (ii) that compensation be paid. This is in addition to the general Art. 43 and Art. 40.3.2° property rights framework. It reflects the drafters' concern to protect the substantial property holdings of religious bodies (particularly the Catholic Church) from arbitrary State acquisition. The provision applies to all religious denominations, not just the Catholic Church.

questions.question: A public authority requires all employees to work on Saturdays, which conflicts with the religious observance of a Jewish employee. Can the employee rely on Art. 44.2.1° to claim a constitutional exemption from Saturday working?

options.A: Yes — Art. 44.2.1° creates an absolute constitutional right not to work on one's religious Sabbath
options.B: Art. 44.2.1° protects freedom of religious practice but does not create an absolute right to workplace accommodation; however, employment equality legislation (Employment Equality Acts 1998-2015) requires reasonable accommodation of religious belief, and courts would interpret the constitutional guarantee as informing that statutory duty; the constitutional dimension strengthens the statutory claim
options.C: Art. 44.2.1° applies only to public worship, not to private religious practice such as Sabbath observance
options.D: The constitutional provision has no application to employment matters — only statutory employment equality law applies

questions.explanation: Art. 44.2.1° protects freedom of conscience and religious practice, which can engage employment situations. However, it is not absolute — it is subject to public order and morality, and must be balanced against operational requirements. The Employment Equality Acts 1998-2015 (s.16) require employers to provide reasonable accommodation for religious beliefs, including scheduling adjustments where operationally feasible. The constitutional guarantee under Art. 44.2.1° informs the interpretation of the statutory reasonable accommodation duty. Courts may also use the constitutional value to resolve private law employment disputes (horizontal indirect effect).

questions.question: In Corway v Independent Newspapers [1999] 4 IR 484, the Supreme Court considered a private prosecution for blasphemy under Art. 40.6.1°(i)'s reference to blasphemy as a criminal offence. What was the outcome?

options.A: The court held that blasphemy is constitutionally required to be criminalised and ordered Parliament to enact a blasphemy law
options.B: The court held that without statutory definition, it was impossible to determine what 'blasphemy' meant in the constitutional provision; the offence was so uncertain that a private prosecution could not proceed — effectively neutering the blasphemy provision pending legislation
options.C: The court upheld the conviction for blasphemy as the Constitution was self-executing
options.D: The court held that blasphemy cannot be a criminal offence as it violates Art. 44.2.1° freedom of religion

questions.explanation: Corway v Independent Newspapers [1999] 4 IR 484 arose from an attempt to prosecute a newspaper for blasphemy. The Supreme Court held that the constitutional reference to blasphemy in Art. 40.6.1°(i) was so vague and undefined — without any statutory definition — that it was impossible to determine what the offence was. The prosecution could not proceed as the offence lacked the certainty required by law. This led to the Defamation Act 2009 (which created a statutory offence of blasphemous libel) and then the 37th Amendment 2018, which removed the reference to blasphemy from the Constitution entirely.

questions.question: Does Article 44 protect the right of religious schools to maintain a religious ethos in their employment practices — for example, by preferring to hire teachers who share the school's religious beliefs?

options.A: No — employment in all schools is subject to full anti-discrimination law without religious exemption
options.B: Yes — Art. 44.2.6° (right to manage own affairs) and Art. 44.2.5° (right to maintain educational institutions) protect the right of religious bodies to maintain their institutional ethos; the Equal Status Acts and Employment Equality Acts contain religion exemptions for institutions with a religious ethos
options.C: Religious schools may only discriminate in employment for directly religious roles (e.g., chaplain) but not for teaching positions
options.D: Art. 44 protections apply only to practices of worship and do not extend to institutional employment matters

questions.explanation: Art. 44.2.6° protects the right of religious denominations to manage their own affairs and maintain educational institutions. This includes the right to maintain the religious ethos of their schools. Section 37 of the Employment Equality Acts 1998-2015 provides an exemption permitting religious, educational or medical institutions to discriminate in employment to maintain their religious ethos. Irish courts and the ECHR (Fernandez Martinez v Spain [2014]) have recognised that religious institutions' autonomy (including employment decisions) can be constitutionally and Convention-protected, subject to proportionality in balancing employees' rights.

questions.question: The State proposes to require all primary school children to attend lessons on comparative religion as part of a revised national curriculum, regardless of parental religious or secular views. Which constitutional provisions are engaged?

options.B: Art. 42.1 (parental right to direct moral and religious education), Art. 44.2.1° (freedom of conscience and religious practice), and Art. 44.2.4° (State not to discriminate on grounds of religion in attending schools) are all engaged; compulsory religious content without opt-out mechanisms could violate parental and individual religious freedom rights
options.C: The curriculum is exclusively a matter for the executive; no constitutional provisions are engaged

questions.explanation: A mandatory comparative religion curriculum engages multiple constitutional provisions: Art. 42.1 (parental right to direct their children's religious and moral education), Art. 44.2.1° (freedom of conscience — including the right of secularists not to be subjected to religious instruction), and Art. 44.2.4° (State may not require school attendance at any institution which does not respect parental religious beliefs or lack thereof). The constitutional resolution typically involves providing opt-out mechanisms, ensuring content is purely factual/comparative (not confessional), and allowing parental choice. Fully compulsory religious instruction without any opt-out would be constitutionally problematic.

questions.question: Article 44.2.4° provides that legislation providing State aid for schools shall not discriminate between schools under the management of different religious denominations. What does this provision require?

options.A: The State must give exactly equal funding to every religious denomination regardless of the number of schools or students
options.B: State aid to schools must be distributed on a non-discriminatory basis as between different religious denominations — schools of different faiths in comparable circumstances must receive comparable treatment; the State cannot fund Catholic schools but refuse equivalent funding to Protestant, Jewish or Muslim schools
options.C: Art. 44.2.4° only applies to schools that existed at the time of the 1937 Constitution
options.D: The provision applies only to secondary schools; primary schools are exempt

questions.explanation: Art. 44.2.4° establishes a non-discrimination principle specifically for State aid to schools — it requires that legislation providing State aid shall not discriminate between schools under the management of different religious denominations, nor between schools under the management of a religious denomination and those not under such management. This means the State cannot provide preferential funding to Catholic schools while denying equivalent funding to Church of Ireland, Jewish, Muslim or Educate Together schools in comparable circumstances. The provision is an equality guarantee specifically in the context of educational funding.
